Long documents. Websites and articles. </p><p style="" data-css="tve-u-1921f1614da">A lot of teaching and learning is focused on reading them ... synthesizing them ... remembering them ... and creating something with them.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f1614db" style="">Sometimes, the sheer number of words in these dense resources keep us from even diving in -- or finding the quick answer that we need.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f1614db" style="">That's where Google's Notebook LM comes in. It becomes an instant expert on the documents you give it.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f1614db" style=""><strong>And it's FREE.</strong> </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f1614db" style="">It'll answer questions. It'll summarize. It'll create new simplified resources based on the original source.</p><p style="" data-css="tve-u-1921f1614de"><strong>Plus, it will create podcast-style audio overviews, video overviews, slides, infographics and more!</strong></p><p style="" data-css="tve-u-1921f1614df">NotebookLM can be a fantastic lesson planning assistant for teachers.</p><p style="" data-css="tve-u-1921f1614e0">And, if used appropriately by students, it could be a great way to reinforce learning and to study.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f1614e1" style="">In this post, I'll describe what NotebookLM is and share 10 important things to know for educators and students. You can load your own notebooks, or you can create a new notebook. </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f16311a" style="">These <a href="https://notebooklm.google/plans" target="_blank">plans / pricing tiers </a>are based on your overall Google subscription level. (This isn't the subscription cost just for NotebookLM; it's the cost for the Google account tier.)</p><ul class=""><li class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f16311a" style=""><strong>Standard (free) version: </strong>100 notebooks, 50 sources per notebook, 500,000 words per source, 50 chat queries per day, 3 audio generations per day, 10 deep research sessions per month</li><li class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f16311a" style=""><strong>Plus ($20/mo) version: </strong>500 notebooks, 100 sources per notebook, 500 chat queries per day, 20 audio generations per day, 20 deep research sessions per day</li><li class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f16311a" style=""><strong>Pro ($50/mo) version: </strong>500 notebooks, 300 sources per notebook, 500 chat queries per day, 20 audio generations per day, 20 deep research sessions per day</li><li class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f16311a" style=""><strong>Ultra ($250/mo) version: </strong>500 notebooks, 600 sources per notebook, 5,000 chat queries per day, 200 audio generations per day, 200 deep research sessions per day</li></ul><p data-css="tve-u-198bdaf0ae8" style=""><strong>You might have the "plus (paid) version" in your school Google domain already! </strong>Just go to <a href="https://notebooklm.google" target="_blank" class="" style="outline: none;">notebooklm.google</a> while logged into your school account.</p></div></div>

	<div class="tve-cb"><div class="thrv_wrapper thrv_text_element"><h2 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca79" style="" id="t-1776888589185">10 important things about NotebookLM for educators and students</h2><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca7a" style="" id="t-1776888589186">1. Students can now use NotebookLM.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4ba" style=""><strong><a href="https://support.google.com/notebooklm/answer/16164461?hl=en&co=GENIE.Platform%3DDesktop" target="_blank" class="" style="outline: currentcolor;">According to Google's help page</a></strong>, NotebookLM is available to all users 13+ through the browser (regardless if they're using it through a Workspace for Education organization). Content policies are stricter for users under 18 years old.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4ba" style="">NotebookLM is available to users of all ages with a Workspace for Education account -- even for students. </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4ba" style=""><a href="https://support.google.com/notebooklm/answer/16337734?sjid=1543302857645460075-NC" target="_blank" class="" style="outline: none;" data-css="tve-u-198bdb78acc">Here's more info about using NotebookLM with a school Google account.</a></p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f262099" style="">Also important to note: Google says NotebookLM won't use your personal data to train its AI models. If you're an enterprise (paid) user or an EDU user, it won't use your uploads or queries -- or the model's responses -- to train AI models (and they won't be reviewed by human reviewers).</p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca7c" style="" id="t-1776888589187">2. Audio summary?? WOW. Use it to reinforce and summarize. </h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4be" style="">The audio summary feature is INCREDIBLE. It turns your source materials into a podcast-style interview you can listen to. </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca7b" style=""><span data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d6" style="font-size: 18px; letter-spacing: -0.3px;">The audio summary can be a FANTASTIC way to review and reinforce, though. Students could listen to it during a commute or while doing any sort of routine task. Or, as a teacher, this could be a MUCH less painful way to get acquainted with a long document.</span></p></div><div class="thrv_wrapper tve_image_caption" data-css="tve-u-1921f3ed56c" style=""><span thrv_text_element"><p style="text-align: center;"><a class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f3f5b57" href="https://drive.google.com/file/d/1HqGGUYZK_4axXTBf3wq9Y2-Xvl6IAxXP/view?usp=sharing" style="outline: none;" target="_blank">Click to listen.</a></p></div><div class="thrv_wrapper thrv_text_element"><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca7e" style="" id="t-1776888589188">3. Create audio summaries and share them with students through Google Drive.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c0" style="">If you love this audio summary feature as much as me, don't let terms of use stand in the way of your students benefitting from it. </p><ul class=""><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c1" style="">Upload sources to NotebookLM. Create an audio summary. Click the three dots and download it.</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c2" style="">Upload that audio summary file to Google Drive (or OneDrive or Dropbox or whatever).</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca80" style="">Share the audio file with students with a link -- or through your learning management system (Google Classroom, Canvas, Schoology, etc.).</li></ul><p data-css="tve-u-1921f206f9e" style="">Again, the audio file itself isn't subject to the terms of use ... just the use of the NotebookLM tool itself.</p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca81" style="" id="t-1776888589189">4. Create study guides for students -- and throw them in Quizlet.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c4" style="">Take your sources that students are supposed to learn from -- PDF files, documents, chapters from a textbook, etc. (You can even copy/paste the transcript of YouTube videos as a "copied text" source.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c5" style="">Then, under "Notebook Guide" at the bottom, choose "Study Guide." It'll create short-answer questions (with an answer key) and a glossary of important terms.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca82" style="">What do you do with that study guide? You might go to Quizlet (quizlet.com) and create a study guide (and related flashcards) with it. </p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca83" style="" id="t-1776888589190">5. NotebookLM is available in 50+ languages.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca84" style="">This is huge. If you want to create resources for students whose first language isn't English, you can just change the output language in the settings for the notebook in NotebookLM. (Then change it back if you want it to go back to English.) </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca84" style="">Also, if you're teaching world languages as a subject, creating resources in the target language could be a great language learning opportunity.</p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca85" style="" id="t-1776888589191">6. Use this to help your human brain -- not to replace it.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c8" style="">Be careful. Use this as a tool just like you should with anything else powered by AI -- to accomplish what you want. If you (or your students) need to be well-acquainted with the details of the source material, don't use it to substitute your brain work.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4c9" style="">For students, this could be a great way to reinforce and review material.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca86" style="">For teachers, it shouldn't be used to make decisions for us by bypassing our valuable human teacher brain.</p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca88" style="" id="t-1776888589192">7. Find keywords and a summary for your sources.</h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca89" style="">If you click on one of the sources you uploaded (in the top left), you'll get the "Source Guide." It summarizes your source. It gives the full text of it. Plus, it identifies key topics. Click any of the topics and it'll start a chat saying "Discuss (key topic you just clicked" to summarize that key topic based on the source.</p><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca8a" style="" id="t-1776888589193">8. Get exactly what you want from sources in the chat. </h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4cb" style="">The chat answers your questions and fulfills your requests within the confines of the sources you provided.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4cc" style="">The sky's the limit here, and you can ...</p><ul class=""><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4cd" style="">Get answers to quick questions</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4cf" style="">Ask what the sources don't cover (what the gaps are)</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d0" style="">Ask for the hardest parts to understand</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca8b" style="">Get an explanation a 10 year old would understand</li></ul><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d1" style="">The chat is powered by Gemini 1.5 Pro, Google's most capable model (as of publication of this post). So you can do a lot more than answer simple questions and get simple summaries. </p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d1" style="">Get creative and fun with it. Here are a few I've tried:</p><ul class=""><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d1" style="">Ask it to write the script for a viral TikTok video about your sources.</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d1" style="">Ask it to create a script for the kids TV show Bluey about your sources.</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f2ee4d1" style="">Ask it to write a haiku about your sources.</li></ul><h3 class="" data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca8c" style="" id="t-1776888589194">9. Rename your items in Studio to make them easier to find. </h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca8d" style="">When I create something in Studio (like an audio overview or an infographic), I have started clicking the three dots button and choosing "Rename." I'll add emojis (like stars) or a short abbreviation (like W1 for week 1) so I can find what I'm looking for more quickly. Below is my notebook I'm using to take a college class. I have renamed all of my infographics so I'll know which module of the course they pertain to.</p></div><div class="thrv_wrapper tve_image_caption" data-css="tve-u-19bfb1ad27f"><span Share your notebooks with others (for shared lesson planning). </h3><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Notebooks are shareable! Click the "Share" button to share a notebook with an individual -- and give them viewer rights or editor rights.</p><p data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Imagine this as an AI-powered collaborative lesson planning space ...</p><ul class=""><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Create a notebook for a chapter or unit you're teaching</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Upload all of the sources you'll need to it</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Share it with colleagues</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Create resources that any of your colleagues can access</li><li data-css="tve-u-1921f17ca90" style="">Type and create notes -- and edit resources created by NotebookLM -- to co-create plans</li></ul></div></div>
</div><div class="thrv_wrapper thrv_contentbox_shortcode thrv-content-box tve-elem-default-pad">
	<div class="tve-content-box-background" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6d2f04a"></div>
	<div class="tve-cb"><div class="thrv_wrapper thrv_text_element"><h2 data-path-to-node="0" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff91c" id="t-1776888589196">10 ways teachers are using NotebookLM in the classroom</h2><p data-css="tve-u-19db6dff91e" data-path-to-node="1" style="">The educator community is already finding inventive ways to weave NotebookLM into the fabric of the school day. It’s not just about doing things faster; it’s about doing things that weren't possible before. It's a hot topic and prompted a great discussion inside of our <a href="https://ditch.circle.so/join?invitation_token=074be617b7f6d604395963983bf252119784033c-5a9f18ec-aa57-4060-967a-17bc445ece1d&_bhlid=15333b3b3fa4d52a9adf8767b232233d6add89a9" target="_blank" class="" style="outline: none;" data-css="tve-u-19db6dcedb8">Ditch That Textbook online community</a> </p><p data-css="tve-u-19db6dff91e" data-path-to-node="1" style=""><em>Note: Are you a member of the DTT community yet? No? Well <a href="https://ditch.circle.so/join?invitation_token=074be617b7f6d604395963983bf252119784033c-5a9f18ec-aa57-4060-967a-17bc445ece1d&_bhlid=15333b3b3fa4d52a9adf8767b232233d6add89a9" target="_blank" class="" style="outline: none;" data-css="tve-u-19db6e30de1">click this link</a> to join us! </em></p><p data-path-to-node="1" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff91f"><strong>Here are some ways teachers are currently using NotebookLM to move the needle.</strong></p><h3 data-css="tve-u-19db6de850d" data-path-to-node="5" style=""><span data-css="tve-u-19db6de850f"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="5">1. Identifying Patterns in Student Data</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="5" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff921"><b data-index-in-node="40" data-path-to-node="5">Deanna Cross</b> uses the tool for high-level data analysis. By uploading spreadsheets from various testing platforms (using student numbers to maintain confidentiality), she asks the AI to find trends and patterns that might be invisible to the naked eye.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="3" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6de3b58"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6de5e95"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="3">2. Navigating Massive Curriculums</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="3" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff922"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="3"></b>When faced with dense curriculum packages, <b data-index-in-node="77" data-path-to-node="3">Kerry Fergason</b> uses the tool to "internalize" the content. It helps her quickly decide which sections to prioritize, where to differentiate, and what can be skipped to better serve her specific students.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="4" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6de5e8b"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6de8511"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="4">3. Building an Interactive Resource Hub</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="4" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff924"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="4"></b><b data-index-in-node="40" data-path-to-node="4">Deb White</b> describes her notebooks as a "Wakelet on steroids." She uses them to organize background knowledge, articles, and assignments in one interactive space where students can search for answers across multiple documents at once.</p><h3 data-css="tve-u-19db6dddf65" data-path-to-node="2" style=""><span data-css="tve-u-19db6ddf215"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="2">4. Anticipating Student Misconceptions</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="2" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff925"><b data-index-in-node="39" data-path-to-node="2">Ariella Pardo</b> uses NotebookLM as a "Thinking Partner." By uploading lesson materials, she can ask the AI to generate guiding questions or predict where students might struggle before the lesson even begins, allowing for proactive planning.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="6" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6de9e40"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6dec0ae"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="6">5. Generating Instant Survey Reports</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="6" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff927"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="6"></b>To get a pulse on the classroom, <b data-index-in-node="70" data-path-to-node="6">Hatice Karaaslan</b> uploads raw student survey responses. NotebookLM then generates compact reports and summary infographics that make student feedback easy to visualize and act upon.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="7" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dec0a4"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6dee963"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="7">6. Hooking Students with Audio</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="7" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff928"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="7"></b>Many teachers are using the <b data-index-in-node="59" data-path-to-node="7">Audio Overview</b> as the perfect "Hook." By creating a 2-minute, podcast-style introduction to a new unit, they can spark immediate interest and curiosity before the heavy reading begins.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="8" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dee95e"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6dee95f"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="8">7. Deepening Literary Analysis</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="8" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff929"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="8"></b><b data-index-in-node="31" data-path-to-node="8">Erich Dorzab</b> has his students "chat" with a novel they’ve just finished. Students use the AI to hunt down specific evidence and quotes to support their claims in essays, turning the search for citations into a conversation.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="9" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6df03f7"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6df2916"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="9">8. Validating Student Creativity</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="9" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff92b"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="9"></b>In a unique twist for creative writing, <b data-index-in-node="73" data-path-to-node="9">Vaughn Jennings</b> has students listen to a generated podcast <i data-index-in-node="132" data-path-to-node="9">about</i> the books they wrote. Hearing "experts" discuss their work gives students a massive sense of professional accomplishment and pride.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="10" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6df290a"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6df290c"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="10">9. Mastering Multimedia Integration</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="10" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff92c"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="10"></b>Power users like <b data-index-in-node="53" data-path-to-node="10">Brent Mischnick</b> and <b data-index-in-node="73" data-path-to-node="10">Don Sturm</b> have mastered the workflow of taking NotebookLM content further. They download the audio files to listen to research during their commutes, or move generated visuals into Canva to polish the design.</p><h3 data-path-to-node="11" class="" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6df5cea"><span style="font-weight: normal;" data-css="tve-u-19db6df5ceb"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="11">10. Creating Instructional Video Material</b></span></h3><p data-path-to-node="11" style="" data-css="tve-u-19db6dff92e"><b data-index-in-node="0" data-path-to-node="11"></b><b data-index-in-node="42" data-path-to-node="11">Karen Szczytko</b> takes the output a step further by generating slide shows within the tool and then converting them into instructional videos using Google Vids. This creates a seamless bridge between raw research and student-facing video lessons.</p></div></div>
</div></div></div><div class="tcb_flag" style="display: none"></div>

	/**
	 * Displays toast message

	 * @param {string}   message  - message to display
	 * @param {Boolean}  error    - whether the message is an error or not
	 * @param {Function} callback - callback function to be called after the message is closed
	 */
	function tcbToast( message, error, callback ) {
		/* Also allow "message" objects */
		if ( typeof message !== 'string' ) {
			message = message.message || message.error || message.success;
		}
		if ( ! error ) {
			error = false;

		TCB_Front.notificationElement.toggle( message, error ? 'error' : 'success', callback );
